flashcards.front: What is the average time complexity of Quick Sort?
flashcards.back: O(n log n). This occurs when the pivot divides the array into roughly two equal halves at each recursive step.

flashcards.front: What is the worst-case time complexity of Quick Sort and when does it occur?
flashcards.back: O(n²). This occurs when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element, resulting in highly unbalanced partitions (e.g., sorted array with first/last element as pivot).

flashcards.front: Why is Quick Sort considered in-place?
flashcards.back: Quick Sort requires only O(log n) auxiliary space for the recursion stack (in the average case). It doesn't require additional merging like Merge Sort; elements are swapped within the original array.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of Quick Sort?
flashcards.back: O(log n) average case for recursion stack. O(n) worst case when recursion becomes unbalanced. Can be reduced to O(log n) worst case using tail call optimization or iterative implementation.

flashcards.front: Is Quick Sort stable? Give reason.
flashcards.back: No, Quick Sort is not stable. During partitioning, elements with equal keys may be swapped across the pivot, changing their relative order.

flashcards.front: What are common pivot selection strategies in Quick Sort?
flashcards.back: 1) First element as pivot 2) Last element as pivot 3) Random element as pivot 4) Median-of-three (first, middle, last) 5) Median-of-three with median as pivot.
Two pointers i and j start at both ends. i moves right finding elements > pivot, j moves left finding elements < pivot. When both stop, swap. Continue until i >= j. Returns j as partition index.
Pivot is placed at end. A smaller index 'i' tracks the boundary of elements smaller than pivot. For each element < pivot, increment i and swap. Finally, place pivot after i. Returns partition index.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Quick Sort and Merge Sort?
flashcards.back: Quick Sort: divides based on pivot, sorts while conquering, in-place, not stable, O(n log n) avg. Merge Sort: divides at midpoint, conquers then merge, not in-place (needs O(n) aux), stable, O(n log n) always.

flashcards.front: What is randomized Quick Sort and why is it used?
flashcards.back: Randomized Quick Sort randomly selects the pivot (typically via random index swap before partitioning). It ensures O(n log n) expected time regardless of input distribution, preventing worst-case behavior on adversarial inputs.

flashcards.front: What is tail call optimization in Quick Sort?
flashcards.back: After partitioning, instead of recursing on both subarrays, recurse on the smaller one first and use iteration (tail call) for the larger one. This limits recursion depth to O(log n) in worst case.

flashcards.front: How many comparisons does Quick Sort make in the worst case?
flashcards.back: In worst case, Quick Sort makes approximately n(n-1)/2 = O(n²) comparisons. In average case with distinct elements, it makes approximately n log n comparisons.
